option: Platform Environment Variable Store (Vercel/Netlify/Cloudflare dashboard or CLI)
best_for: Any project deployed to a managed platform — store real secrets there, scoped per environment (production/preview/development), never in a committed file
avoid_when: A secret shared across many services/teams needing centralized rotation and audit logging beyond what a single hosting platform's env store offers — a dedicated secrets manager fits better there
tradeoffs: Simple, git-integrated, and scoped per environment out of the box, but secrets live inside that one platform's account — factor that into your access-control/offboarding process

option: Dedicated Secrets Manager (e.g. a cloud secrets-manager service)
best_for: Larger orgs needing centralized secret rotation, fine-grained access policies, and audit logs across multiple services/platforms, not just one hosting provider
avoid_when: Small projects on a single managed platform — the platform's built-in env store already covers the need without extra infrastructure
tradeoffs: Centralized rotation, versioning, and audit trail across every service that needs a given secret, at the cost of extra integration work (fetching secrets at runtime/build time from the manager) compared to a platform's native env store

best_for: Every project, without exception — document every required variable's name and purpose in a committed template, keep real values local-only and out of git
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — this is the baseline convention that makes onboarding a new developer possible without them guessing which env vars exist
tradeoffs: New developers get a complete, accurate list of required configuration without ever seeing real secret values; keep `.env.example` in sync with reality whenever a new required variable is added

option: Never Commit a Real Secret, Even Temporarily
best_for: Every commit, every branch — a secret committed even once and later removed is still in git history and must be treated as compromised and rotated
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — 'I'll remove it in the next commit' does not remove it from history; use git-secrets/pre-commit hooks to catch this before it happens, not after
tradeoffs: A leaked secret in git history requires rotation regardless of whether the commit was later reverted — prevention (pre-commit scanning) is far cheaper than remediation (full credential rotation)

option: Validate All Required Env Vars at Startup (fail fast)
best_for: Every service — check that every required variable exists and is well-formed before accepting traffic, crashing immediately if not (see BE081)
avoid_when: Never skip — a missing env var surfacing as a confusing runtime error hours later, deep in a code path, is far harder to diagnose than an immediate startup crash with a clear message
tradeoffs: Turns 'mysterious runtime failure' into 'obvious deploy-time failure with a clear error message,' which is dramatically easier to debug and catch in CI before it ever reaches production

option: Same Config Shape Across dev/staging/prod, Different Values
best_for: Every project — the same typed config schema validates all three environments; only the actual values differ, never the required-variable list
avoid_when: A genuinely environment-specific feature flag or behavior toggle — that's a deliberate conditional in code, not an excuse to let the env var schema itself diverge silently between environments
tradeoffs: Prevents 'works in staging, breaks in prod because a var was never set there' by making the schema itself the single source of truth for what every environment needs

option: Distinguish Build-Time-Baked vs Runtime-Read Env Vars
best_for: Frontend frameworks (Next.js `NEXT_PUBLIC_*`, Vite `VITE_*`) where some env vars get baked into the client bundle at build time and others are read server-side at runtime — know which is which for every variable
avoid_when: Never confuse the two — a secret accidentally given a build-time-public prefix ships directly into the client-visible JavaScript bundle
tradeoffs: Build-time-public variables are visible to anyone who opens browser devtools — never put a real secret behind a `NEXT_PUBLIC_`/`VITE_`-style prefix; only genuinely public config (a public API base URL, a publishable key) belongs there

option: Server-Only Secrets Never Exposed to Client Bundle
best_for: Any secret (database credentials, private API keys, service role keys) — keep them server-side only, read via a server-only env var with no public prefix, never passed as a prop into client-rendered code
avoid_when: A value that's genuinely meant to be public (a publishable/anon key designed for client-side use, like Stripe's publishable key or Supabase's anon key) — those are safe by design to expose
tradeoffs: The single most common real-world leak of this category is a secret key accidentally treated the same as a publishable key and shipped to the browser — always double check which key type a given credential actually is before exposing it client-side

option: Rotate Secrets on a Schedule and Immediately After Any Suspected Exposure
best_for: Every credential with real access (database passwords, service role keys, third-party API keys) — rotate periodically as a baseline hygiene practice, and immediately, unconditionally, after any suspected leak
avoid_when: Never skip the immediate rotation after a suspected leak specifically — 'we're not sure it was actually exposed' is not a reason to delay; rotate first, investigate after
tradeoffs: Limits the window of usefulness for a credential that leaks despite prevention efforts (DO046) — treat rotation capability as something to build/verify before you need it urgently, not scramble to figure out during an actual incident

best_for: Every credential — a CI deploy token, a database user, a third-party API key should have only the specific permissions the thing using it actually needs, scoped separately per environment (a staging DB credential should not also work against production)
avoid_when: A genuinely single-environment, single-purpose internal tool where separate scoping would be pure ceremony with no real risk reduction
tradeoffs: A leaked staging credential should never be able to touch production data — least-privilege scoping is what makes that guarantee true instead of aspirational

option: Separate API Keys per Environment for Every Third-Party Service
best_for: Stripe, Shopify, email providers, analytics — every third-party integration should have distinct dev/staging/production keys/accounts wherever the provider supports it
avoid_when: A provider that genuinely offers no environment separation (rare) — document that limitation explicitly and add extra caution around testing against it
tradeoffs: Prevents a staging/test run from accidentally sending real emails, charging real cards, or polluting production analytics — Stripe and most payment providers explicitly support and expect separate test-mode keys for exactly this reason

option: CI Pipeline Secrets Scoped Separately From Runtime App Secrets
best_for: Deploy tokens, CI-only service credentials — these are a distinct credential set from the application's own runtime environment variables, even when both eventually configure the same deployed service
avoid_when: Treating the CI deploy token and the app's runtime database password as interchangeable or stored in the same place with the same access scope — they serve different purposes and should be scoped/audited independently
tradeoffs: Keeps a compromised CI pipeline from automatically having the same blast radius as a compromised running application, since the two credential sets are genuinely separate

option: Document Where Each Secret Comes From and Who Can Rotate It
best_for: Every project with more than a couple of secrets — a short internal doc (or well-commented `.env.example`) noting which third-party dashboard issued each credential and who on the team has access to regenerate it
avoid_when: A genuinely single-developer project where that context already lives entirely in one person's head — still worth writing down before the project has a second contributor
tradeoffs: Prevents a rotation-under-pressure scenario (post-incident, or an offboarding developer) from turning into an investigation of 'wait, where did this key even come from'
